name: Frank Rehak
abstract: Frank Rehak (July 6, 1926, in New York City, New York – June 22, 1987, in Badger, California) was an American jazz trombonist. He began on piano and cello before switching to trombone.

concerts.label: Miles Davis with Gil Evans and Orchestra

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Woods
about_quotes.evidence: there was a few of us: Frank Rehak, Marty Flax and myself, the three white guys on the Dizzy band
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Woods — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: I was added to a trombone section that had Frank Rehak and Bobby Brookmeyer. ... I remember Bobby Brookmeyer and Frank Rehak came up to me and said, “Tell George these parts are almost impossible to play.
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: Rehak. Yeah, Rehak – were facing us... The trumpets were behind them. Then there were two other woodwinds over there with the trombones... I was hired, and I played. Then, I became one of his favorite horn players, along with Julius Watkins and Willie Ruff.
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Woody Herman
about_quotes.evidence: Frank Rehak, yes, of course, and several others. ... we had people come through the band like Kai Winding and people like that.

about_quotes.speaker: Curtis Fuller
about_quotes.evidence: We would meet at Frank Rehak's studio across from Birdland, and we were developing this brass section.
about_quotes.doc_title: Curtis Fuller — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Melba Liston
about_quotes.evidence: One you had Slide Hampton, Frank Rehak. ... That was a different Company altogether. ... we had a date with him and the 4 'bones too.
about_quotes.doc_title: Melba Liston — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
